metadata.title: Quick-fire: After the breach
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Sixth reciprocity-cooperation quick-fire. Prior rounds covered when-to-engage (qf-001), pattern-vs-moment tension (qf-003), asymmetric-reciprocity (qf-005), commitment-keeping (qf-007), pay-it-forward (qf-009). This round centers the breach-response question: when cooperation is broken (by mistake, neglect, or low-grade dishonesty), what does the user do next? Axelrod 1984 showed tit-for-tat-with-forgiveness is the dominant strategy in iterated games, but real human reciprocity has gradations. Specifically tests whether revealed forgiveness is uniform across breach types or fragments by counterparty / breach-severity / repetition.
metadata.domain_anchor: Axelrod 1984 The Evolution of Cooperation; McCullough et al. 2013 'Cognitive systems for revenge and forgiveness' on forgiveness as adaptive and context-dependent; Rusbult's investment-model literature on relationship-specific forgiveness thresholds.

items.prompt: A friend backs out of a plan you both committed to, last minute, with a vague excuse you don't fully buy. It's the first time they've done this; the cost to you is moderate but real.

options.text: Accept it warmly: 'Things come up. Let's reschedule.' First-time, low-evidence-of-pattern — extend trust.
Name the gap honestly: 'No worries on the reschedule, but the excuse felt off — anything actually going on?'

items.prompt: A colleague was misleading in a small way that affected you (mis-attributed credit, claimed a deliverable wasn't ready when it was, etc.). The pattern is new. They haven't apologized.

options.text: Move on. One instance isn't a pattern; raising it could escalate something that wasn't intentional.
Quietly adjust your trust calibration: stay cooperative on the surface, double-check shared work, raise it if it happens again.

items.prompt: A family member apologizes for a hurt that's still recent — within the past few months. The apology is genuine but the apology alone doesn't undo the hurt; you can feel that you haven't fully landed back yet.

options.text: Accept the apology fully and name that you accept it. The completed apology has its own work to do; protecting yourself from it slows the repair.
Accept the apology partially and honestly: 'I appreciate this and want to be able to fully accept it; I'm not there yet. I'll let you know when I am.'

items.prompt: Someone you'd helped substantively in the past now does not reciprocate when you make a similar ask. They have a passable reason. The asymmetry stings.

options.text: Let it go. Reciprocity isn't a ledger; the help you gave was for its own reasons, and one non-reciprocation isn't a pattern.
Name it once, plainly: 'I notice the asymmetry; I'm not keeping a ledger but I wanted you to know I noticed.'

items.prompt: A repeat-pattern. Same friend who broke commitment first time (light forgiveness in i01-style) has now done it again, third time in six months. The excuses have stayed vague.

options.text: Stay generous. Some friends just have less reliable lives; the friendship is bigger than the cancellations.
Renegotiate the relationship's reliability explicitly. 'I've noticed a pattern — let's talk about what's actually realistic for both of us.'

items.prompt: A colleague who had agreed with you privately on a position publicly contradicted you in a meeting. After the meeting, they haven't reached out about it. Two days have passed.

options.text: Let it go and don't reach out. The public dynamic is its own logic; raising it after the fact will be read as keeping score.
Reach out. 'I'd value understanding what shifted between our private conversation and the meeting — not as a complaint, just so I know where we stand.'
